name: pytest-testinfra

metadata.name: pytest-testinfra
classifiers: License :: OSI Approved :: Apache Software License

documentation:
# Testinfra test your infrastructure

Latest documentation: <https://testinfra.readthedocs.io/en/latest>

## About

With Testinfra you can write unit tests in Python to test *actual state* of
your servers configured by management tools like [Salt], [Ansible], [Puppet],
[Chef] and so on.

Testinfra aims to be a [Serverspec] equivalent in python and is written as
a plugin to the powerful [Pytest] test engine

## License

[Apache License 2.0](https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest-testinfra/blob/main/LICENSE)

The logo is licensed under the [Creative Commons NoDerivatives 4.0 License](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nd/4.0/)
If you have some other use in mind, contact us.

## Quick start

Install testinfra using pip:

```
$ pip install pytest-testinfra

# or install the devel version
$ pip install 'git+https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest-testinfra@main#egg=pytest-testinfra'
```

Write your first tests file to `test_myinfra.py`:

```python
def test_passwd_file(host):
    passwd = host.file("/etc/passwd")
    assert passwd.contains("root")
    assert passwd.user == "root"
    assert passwd.group == "root"
    assert passwd.mode == 0o644


def test_nginx_is_installed(host):
    nginx = host.package("nginx")
    assert nginx.is_installed
    assert nginx.version.startswith("1.2")


def test_nginx_running_and_enabled(host):
    nginx = host.service("nginx")
    assert nginx.is_running
    assert nginx.is_enabled
```

And run it:

```
$ py.test -v test_myinfra.py


====================== test session starts ======================
platform linux -- Python 2.7.3 -- py-1.4.26 -- pytest-2.6.4
plugins: testinfra
collected 3 items

test_myinfra.py::test_passwd_file[local] PASSED
test_myinfra.py::test_nginx_is_installed[local] PASSED
test_myinfra.py::test_nginx_running_and_enabled[local] PASSED

=================== 3 passed in 0.66 seconds ====================
```

[ansible]: https://www.ansible.com/
[chef]: https://www.chef.io/
[puppet]: https://puppetlabs.com/
[pytest]: https://pytest.org/
[salt]: https://saltstack.com/
[serverspec]: https://serverspec.org/

classes.name: testinfra.modules.salt.Salt
documentation:
Run salt module functions

```pycon
>>> host.salt("pkg.version", "nginx")
'1.6.2-5'
>>> host.salt("pkg.version", ["nginx", "php5-fpm"])
{'nginx': '1.6.2-5', 'php5-fpm': '5.6.7+dfsg-1'}
>>> host.salt("grains.item", ["osarch", "mem_total", "num_cpus"])
{'osarch': 'amd64', 'num_cpus': 4, 'mem_total': 15520}
```

Run `salt-call sys.doc` to get a complete list of functions

classes.name: testinfra.modules.supervisor.Supervisor

methods.name: testinfra.modules.supervisor.Supervisor.is_running
documentation: Return True if managed service is in status RUNNING

methods.name: testinfra.modules.supervisor.Supervisor.status
documentation:
Return the status of the managed service

Status can be STOPPED, STARTING, RUNNING, BACKOFF, STOPPING,
EXITED, FATAL, UNKNOWN.

See http://supervisord.org/subprocess.html#process-states

methods.name: testinfra.modules.supervisor.Supervisor.pid
documentation: Return the pid (as int) of the managed service

methods.name: testinfra.modules.supervisor.Supervisor.get_services
documentation:
Get a list of services running under supervisor

```pycon
>>> host.supervisor.get_services()
[<Supervisor(name="gunicorn", status="RUNNING", pid=4232)>
 <Supervisor(name="celery", status="FATAL", pid=None)>]
```

The path where supervisorctl and its configuration file reside can be specified.

```pycon
>>> host.supervisor.get_services("/usr/bin/supervisorctl", "/etc/supervisor/supervisord.conf")
[<Supervisor(name="gunicorn", status="RUNNING", pid=4232)>
 <Supervisor(name="celery", status="FATAL", pid=None)>]
```

Test supervisor managed services

```pycon
>>> gunicorn = host.supervisor("gunicorn")
>>> gunicorn.status
'RUNNING'
>>> gunicorn.is_running
True
>>> gunicorn.pid
4242
```

The path where supervisorctl and its configuration file reside can be specified.

```pycon
>>> gunicorn = host.supervisor("gunicorn", "/usr/bin/supervisorctl", "/etc/supervisor/supervisord.conf")
>>> gunicorn.status
'RUNNING'
```

classes.name: testinfra.modules.docker.Docker

methods.name: testinfra.modules.docker.Docker.client_version
documentation: Docker client version

methods.name: testinfra.modules.docker.Docker.server_version
documentation: Docker server version

methods.name: testinfra.modules.docker.Docker.version
documentation:
Docker [version] with an optional format (Go template).

```pycon
>>> host.docker.version()
Client: Docker Engine - Community
...
>>> host.docker.version("{{.Client.Context}}"))
default
```

[version]: https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/commandline/version/

methods.name: testinfra.modules.docker.Docker.get_containers
documentation:
Return a list of containers

By default return list of all containers, including non-running
containers.

Filtering can be done using filters keys defined on
<https://docs.docker.com/engine/reference/commandline/ps/#filtering>

Multiple filters for a given key is handled by giving a list of string
as value.

```pycon
>>> host.docker.get_containers()
[<docker nginx>, <docker redis>, <docker app>]
# Get all running containers
>>> host.docker.get_containers(status="running")
[<docker app>]
# Get containers named "nginx"
>>> host.docker.get_containers(name="nginx")
[<docker nginx>]
# Get containers named "nginx" or "redis"
>>> host.docker.get_containers(name=["nginx", "redis"])
[<docker nginx>, <docker redis>]
```

Test docker containers running on system.

Example:

```pycon
>>> nginx = host.docker("app_nginx")
>>> nginx.is_running
True
>>> nginx.id
'7e67dc7495ca8f451d346b775890bdc0fb561ecdc97b68fb59ff2f77b509a8fe'
>>> nginx.name
'app_nginx'
```

classes.name: testinfra.modules.mountpoint.MountPoint

methods.name: testinfra.modules.mountpoint.MountPoint.exists
documentation:
Return True if the mountpoint exists

```pycon
>>> host.mount_point("/").exists
True
```

```pycon
>>> host.mount_point("/not/a/mountpoint").exists
False
```

methods.name: testinfra.modules.mountpoint.MountPoint.filesystem
documentation:
Returns the filesystem type associated

```pycon
>>> host.mount_point("/").filesystem
'ext4'
```

methods.name: testinfra.modules.mountpoint.MountPoint.device
documentation:
Return the device associated

```pycon
>>> host.mount_point("/").device
'/dev/sda1'
```

methods.name: testinfra.modules.mountpoint.MountPoint.options
documentation:
Return a list of options that a mount point has been created with

```pycon
>>> host.mount_point("/").options
['rw', 'relatime', 'data=ordered']
```

methods.name: testinfra.modules.mountpoint.MountPoint.get_mountpoints
documentation:
Returns a list of MountPoint instances

```pycon
>>> host.mount_point.get_mountpoints()
[<MountPoint(path=/proc, device=proc, filesystem=proc, options=rw,nosuid,nodev,noexec,relatime)>,
 <MountPoint(path=/, device=/dev/sda1, filesystem=ext4, options=rw,relatime,errors=remount-ro,data=ordered)>]
```
Test Mount Points

classes.name: testinfra.modules.package.Package

methods.name: testinfra.modules.package.Package.is_installed
documentation:
Test if the package is installed

```pycon
>>> host.package("nginx").is_installed
True
```

Supported package systems:

- apk (Alpine)
- apt (Debian, Ubuntu, ...)
- brew (macOS)
- pacman (Arch, Manjaro )
- pkg (FreeBSD)
- pkg_info (NetBSD)
- pkg_info (OpenBSD)
- rpm (RHEL, RockyLinux, Fedora, ...)

methods.name: testinfra.modules.package.Package.release
documentation:
Return the release specific info from the package version

```pycon
>>> host.package("nginx").release
'1.el6'
```

methods.name: testinfra.modules.package.Package.version
documentation:
Return package version as returned by the package system

```pycon
>>> host.package("nginx").version
'1.2.1-2.2+wheezy3'
```
Test packages status and version

classes.name: testinfra.modules.sudo.Sudo
documentation:
Sudo module allow to run certain portion of code under another user.

It is used as a context manager and can be nested.

```pycon
>>> Command.check_output("whoami")
'phil'
>>> with host.sudo():
...     host.check_output("whoami")
...     with host.sudo("www-data"):
...         host.check_output("whoami")
...
'root'
'www-data'
```

methods.name: testinfra.modules.service.Service.exists
documentation: Test if service is exists

documentation: Test if service is running

methods.name: testinfra.modules.service.Service.is_enabled
documentation: Test if service is enabled

methods.name: testinfra.modules.service.Service.is_valid
documentation:
Test if service is valid

This method is only available in the systemd implementation,
it will raise `NotImplementedError` in others implementation

methods.name: testinfra.modules.service.Service.is_masked
documentation:
Test if service is masked

This method is only available in the systemd implementation,
it will raise `NotImplementedError` in others implementations

methods.name: testinfra.modules.service.Service.systemd_properties
documentation:
Properties of the service (unit).

Return service properties as a `dict`,
empty properties are not returned.

```pycon
>>> ntp = host.service("ntp")
>>> ntp.systemd_properties["FragmentPath"]
'/lib/systemd/system/ntp.service'
```

This method is only available in the systemd implementation,
it will raise `NotImplementedError` in others implementations

Note: based on [systemctl show]

[systemctl show]: https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man1/systemctl.1.html

Test services

Implementations:

- Linux: detect Systemd, Upstart or OpenRC, fallback to SysV
- FreeBSD: service(1)
- OpenBSD: `/etc/rc.d/$name check` for `is_running`
  `rcctl ls on` for `is_enabled` (only OpenBSD >= 5.8)
- NetBSD: `/etc/rc.d/$name onestatus` for `is_running`
  (`is_enabled` is not yet implemented)

methods.name: testinfra.modules.service.SystemdService._has_systemd_suffix
documentation: Check if service name has a known systemd unit suffix

class_variables.name: testinfra.modules.service.SystemdService.suffix_list
documentation:
List of valid suffixes for systemd unit files

See systemd.unit(5) for more details

classes.name: testinfra.modules.podman.Podman

methods.name: testinfra.modules.podman.Podman.get_containers
documentation:
Return a list of containers

By default return list of all containers, including non-running
containers.

Filtering can be done using filters keys defined in
podman-ps(1).

Multiple filters for a given key is handled by giving a list of string
as value.

```pycon
>>> host.podman.get_containers()
[<podman nginx>, <podman redis>, <podman app>]
# Get all running containers
>>> host.podman.get_containers(status="running")
[<podman app>]
# Get containers named "nginx"
>>> host.podman.get_containers(name="nginx")
[<podman nginx>]
# Get containers named "nginx" or "redis"
>>> host.podman.get_containers(name=["nginx", "redis"])
[<podman nginx>, <podman redis>]
```

Test podman containers running on system.

Example:

```pycon
>>> nginx = host.podman("app_nginx")
>>> nginx.is_running
True
>>> nginx.id
'7e67dc7495ca8f451d346b775890bdc0fb561ecdc97b68fb59ff2f77b509a8fe'
>>> nginx.name
'app_nginx'
```

classes.name: testinfra.modules.process.Process

methods.name: testinfra.modules.process.Process.filter
documentation:
Get a list of matching process

```pycon
>>> host.process.filter(user="root", comm="zsh")
[<process zsh (pid=2715)>, <process zsh (pid=10502)>, ...]
```

methods.name: testinfra.modules.process.Process.get
documentation:
Get one matching process

Raise `RuntimeError` if no process found or multiple process
matching filters.

Test Processes attributes

Processes are selected using `filter()` or `get()`, attributes names
are described in the [ps(1) man page](http://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man1/ps.1.html#STANDARD_FORMATSPECIFIERS).

```pycon
>>> master = host.process.get(user="root", comm="nginx")
# Here is the master nginx process (running as root)
>>> master.args
'nginx: master process /usr/sbin/nginx -g daemon on; master_process on;'
# Here are the worker processes (Parent PID = master PID)
>>> workers = host.process.filter(ppid=master.pid)
>>> len(workers)
4
# Nginx don't eat memory
>>> sum([w.pmem for w in workers])
0.8
# But php does !
>>> sum([p.pmem for p in host.process.filter(comm="php5-fpm")])
19.2
```

methods.name: testinfra.modules.process.PosixProcess._get_processes
documentation: Should be portable on both Linux and BSD

classes.name: testinfra.modules.iptables.Iptables

methods.name: testinfra.modules.iptables.Iptables.rules
documentation:
Returns list of iptables rules

> Based on output of `iptables -t TABLE -S CHAIN` command
>
> > optionally takes takes the following arguments:
> > : - table: defaults to `filter`
> >   - chain: defaults to all chains
> >   - version: default 4 (iptables), optionally 6 (ip6tables)

```pycon
>>> host.iptables.rules()
[
    '-P INPUT ACCEPT',
    '-P FORWARD ACCEPT',
    '-P OUTPUT ACCEPT',
    '-A INPUT -i lo -j ACCEPT',
    '-A INPUT -j REJECT'
    '-A FORWARD -j REJECT'
]
>>> host.iptables.rules("nat", "INPUT")
['-P PREROUTING ACCEPT']
```
Test iptables rule exists

classes.name: testinfra.modules.ansible.AnsibleException
documentation:
Exception raised when an error occur in an ansible call

result from ansible can be accessed through the `result` attribute

```pycon
>>> try:
...     host.ansible("command", "echo foo")
... except host.ansible.AnsibleException as exc:
...     assert exc.result['failed'] is True
...     assert exc.result['msg'] == 'Skipped. You might want to try check=False'  # noqa
```

classes.name: testinfra.modules.ansible.Ansible

methods.name: testinfra.modules.ansible.Ansible.get_variables
documentation:
Returns a dict of ansible variables

```pycon
>>> host.ansible.get_variables()
{
    'inventory_hostname': 'localhost',
    'group_names': ['ungrouped'],
    'foo': 'bar',
}
```

Run Ansible module functions

This module is only available with the {ref}`ansible connection
backend` connection backend.

[Check mode](https://docs.ansible.com/ansible/playbooks_checkmode.html) is
enabled by default, you can disable it with `check=False`.

[Become](https://docs.ansible.com/ansible/user_guide/become.html#id1) is
`False` by default. You can enable it with `become=True`.

Ansible arguments that are not related to the Ansible inventory or
connection (both managed by testinfra) are also accepted through keyword
arguments:

> - `become_method` *str* sudo, su, doas, etc.
> - `become_user` *str* become this user.
> - `diff` *bool*: when changing (small) files and templates, show the
>   differences in those files.
> - `extra_vars` *dict* serialized to a JSON string, passed to
>   Ansible.
> - `one_line` *bool*: condense output.
> - `user` *str* connect as this user.
> - `verbose` *int* level of verbosity

```pycon
>>> host.ansible("apt", "name=nginx state=present")["changed"]
False
>>> host.ansible("apt", "name=nginx state=present", become=True)["changed"]
False
>>> host.ansible("command", "echo foo", check=False)["stdout"]
'foo'
>>> host.ansible("setup")["ansible_facts"]["ansible_lsb"]["codename"]
'jessie'
>>> host.ansible("file", "path=/etc/passwd")["mode"]
'0640'
>>> host.ansible(
... "command",
... "id --user --name",
... check=False,
... become=True,
... become_user="http",
... )["stdout"]
'http'
>>> host.ansible(
... "apt",
... "name={{ packages }}",
... check=False,
... extra_vars={"packages": ["neovim", "vim"]},
... )
# Installs neovim and vim.
```

documentation: Return network namespace

methods.name: testinfra.modules.addr.Addr._prefix
documentation: Return the prefix to use for commands

methods.name: testinfra.modules.addr.Addr.namespace_exists
documentation: Test if the network namespace exists

methods.name: testinfra.modules.addr.Addr.is_resolvable
documentation: Return if address is resolvable

methods.name: testinfra.modules.addr.Addr.is_reachable
documentation: Return if address is reachable

methods.name: testinfra.modules.addr.Addr.ip_addresses
documentation: Return IP addresses of host

methods.name: testinfra.modules.addr.Addr.ipv4_addresses
documentation: Return IPv4 addresses of host

methods.name: testinfra.modules.addr.Addr.ipv6_addresses
documentation: Return IPv6 addresses of host

methods.name: testinfra.modules.addr.Addr.port
documentation: Return address-port pair

Test remote address

Example:

```pycon
>>> google = host.addr("google.com")
>>> google.is_resolvable
True
>>> '173.194.32.225' in google.ipv4_addresses
True
>>> google.is_reachable
True
>>> google.port(443).is_reachable
True
>>> google.port(666).is_reachable
False
```

Can also be use within a network [namespace].

```pycon
>>> localhost = host.addr("localhost", "ns1")
>>> localhost.is_resolvable
True
```

Network namespaces can only be used if [ip] command is available
because in this case, the module use [ip-netns] as command prefix.
In the other case, it will raise NotImplementedError.

[ip]: https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man8/ip.8.html
[ip-netns]: https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man8/ip-netns.8.html
[namespace]: https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man7/namespaces.7.html

functions.name: testinfra.modules.puppet.parse_puppet_resource
documentation:
Parse data returned by 'puppet resource'

$ puppet resource user
user { 'root':
    ensure  => 'present',
    comment => 'root',
    gid     => '0',
    home    => '/root',
    shell   => '/usr/bin/zsh',
    uid     => '0',
}
user { 'sshd':
  ensure => 'present',
  gid    => '65534',
  home   => '/var/run/sshd',
  shell  => '/usr/sbin/nologin',
  uid    => '106',
}
[...]

classes.name: testinfra.modules.puppet.PuppetResource
documentation:
Get puppet resources

Run `puppet resource --types` to get a list of available types.

```pycon
>>> host.puppet_resource("user", "www-data")
{
    'www-data': {
        'ensure': 'present',
        'comment': 'www-data',
        'gid': '33',
        'home': '/var/www',
        'shell': '/usr/sbin/nologin',
        'uid': '33',
    },
}
```

classes.name: testinfra.modules.puppet.Facter
documentation:
Get facts with [facter](https://puppetlabs.com/facter)

```pycon
>>> host.facter()
{
    "operatingsystem": "Debian",
    "kernel": "linux",
    [...]
}
>>> host.facter("kernelversion", "is_virtual")
{
  "kernelversion": "3.16.0",
  "is_virtual": "false"
}
```

classes.name: testinfra.modules.file.File

methods.name: testinfra.modules.file.File.exists
documentation:
Test if file exists

```pycon
>>> host.file("/etc/passwd").exists
True
>>> host.file("/nonexistent").exists
False
```

methods.name: testinfra.modules.file.File.is_file
documentation: Test if the path is a regular file

methods.name: testinfra.modules.file.File.is_directory
documentation: Test if the path exists and a directory

methods.name: testinfra.modules.file.File.is_executable
documentation: Test if the path exists and permission to execute is granted

methods.name: testinfra.modules.file.File.is_pipe
documentation: Test if the path exists and is a pipe

methods.name: testinfra.modules.file.File.is_socket
documentation: Test if the path exists and is a socket

methods.name: testinfra.modules.file.File.is_symlink
documentation: Test if the path exists and is a symbolic link

methods.name: testinfra.modules.file.File.linked_to
documentation:
Resolve symlink

```pycon
>>> host.file("/var/lock").linked_to
'/run/lock'
```

methods.name: testinfra.modules.file.File.user
documentation:
Return file owner as string

```pycon
>>> host.file("/etc/passwd").user
'root'
```

methods.name: testinfra.modules.file.File.uid
documentation:
Return file user id as integer

```pycon
>>> host.file("/etc/passwd").uid
0
```

methods.name: testinfra.modules.file.File.group
documentation: Return file group name as string

methods.name: testinfra.modules.file.File.gid
documentation: Return file group id as integer

methods.name: testinfra.modules.file.File.mode
documentation:
Return file mode as octal integer

```pycon
>>> host.file("/etc/shadow").mode
416  # Oo640 octal
>>> host.file("/etc/shadow").mode == 0o640
True
>>> oct(host.file("/etc/shadow").mode) == '0o640'
True
```

You can also utilize the file mode constants from
the [stat] library for testing file mode.

```pycon
>>> import stat
>>> host.file("/etc/shadow").mode == stat.S_IRUSR | stat.S_IWUSR | stat.S_IRGRP
True
```

[oct(x)]: https://docs.python.org/3/library/functions.html#oct
[stat]: https://docs.python.org/3/library/stat.html

methods.name: testinfra.modules.file.File.contains
documentation:
Checks content of file for pattern

This uses grep and thus follows the grep regex syntax.

methods.name: testinfra.modules.file.File.sha256sum

methods.name: testinfra.modules.pip.Pip.is_installed
documentation:
Test if the package is installed

```pycon
>>> host.package("pip").is_installed
True
```

methods.name: testinfra.modules.pip.Pip.version
documentation:
Return package version as returned by pip

```pycon
>>> host.package("pip").version
'18.1'
```

methods.name: testinfra.modules.pip.Pip.check
documentation:
Verify installed packages have compatible dependencies.

```pycon
>>> cmd = host.pip.check()
>>> cmd.rc
0
>>> cmd.stdout
No broken requirements found.
```

Can only be used if [pip check] command is available,
for pip versions >= [9.0.0].

[9.0.0]: https://pip.pypa.io/en/stable/news/#id526
[pip check]: https://pip.pypa.io/en/stable/reference/pip_check/

methods.name: testinfra.modules.pip.Pip.get_packages
documentation:
Get all installed packages and versions returned by `pip list`:

```pycon
>>> host.pip.get_packages(pip_path='~/venv/website/bin/pip')
{'Django': {'version': '1.10.2'},
 'mywebsite': {'version': '1.0a3', 'path': '/srv/website'},
 'psycopg2': {'version': '2.6.2'}}
```

methods.name: testinfra.modules.pip.Pip.get_outdated_packages
documentation:
Get all outdated packages with current and latest version

```pycon
>>> host.pip.get_outdated_packages(
...     pip_path='~/venv/website/bin/pip')
{'Django': {'current': '1.10.2', 'latest': '1.10.3'}}
```
Test pip package manager and packages

classes.name: testinfra.modules.interface.Interface

methods.name: testinfra.modules.interface.Interface.addresses
documentation:
Return ipv4 and ipv6 addresses on the interface

```pycon
>>> host.interface("eth0").addresses
['192.168.31.254', '192.168.31.252', 'fe80::e291:f5ff:fe98:6b8c']
```

methods.name: testinfra.modules.interface.Interface.link
documentation:
Return the link properties associated with the interface.

```pycon
>>> host.interface("lo").link
{'address': '00:00:00:00:00:00',
'broadcast': '00:00:00:00:00:00',
'flags': ['LOOPBACK', 'UP', 'LOWER_UP'],
'group': 'default',
'ifindex': 1,
'ifname': 'lo',
'link_type': 'loopback',
'linkmode': 'DEFAULT',
'mtu': 65536,
'operstate': 'UNKNOWN',
'qdisc': 'noqueue',
'txqlen': 1000}
```

methods.name: testinfra.modules.interface.Interface.routes
documentation:
Return the routes associated with the interface, optionally filtered by scope
("host", "link" or "global").

```pycon
>>> host.interface("eth0").routes()
[{'dst': 'default',
'flags': [],
'gateway': '192.0.2.1',
'metric': 3003,
'prefsrc': '192.0.2.5',
'protocol': 'dhcp'},
{'dst': '192.0.2.0/24',
'flags': [],
'metric': 3003,
'prefsrc': '192.0.2.5',
'protocol': 'dhcp',
'scope': 'link'}]
```

methods.name: testinfra.modules.interface.Interface.names
documentation:
Return the names of all the interfaces.

```pycon
>>> host.interface.names()
['lo', 'tunl0', 'ip6tnl0', 'eth0']
```

methods.name: testinfra.modules.interface.Interface.default
documentation:
Return the interface used for the default route.

```pycon
>>> host.interface.default()
<interface eth0>
```

Optionally, the protocol family to use can be enforced.

```pycon
>>> host.interface.default("inet6")
None
```

Test network interfaces

```pycon
>>> host.interface("eth0").exists
True
```

Optionally, the protocol family to use can be enforced.

```pycon
>>> host.interface("eth0", "inet6").addresses
['fe80::e291:f5ff:fe98:6b8c']
```

classes.name: testinfra.modules.blockdevice.BlockDevice

methods.name: testinfra.modules.blockdevice.BlockDevice.is_partition
documentation:
Return True if the device is a partition.

```pycon
>>> host.block_device("/dev/sda1").is_partition
True
```

```pycon
>>> host.block_device("/dev/sda").is_partition
False
```

methods.name: testinfra.modules.blockdevice.BlockDevice.size
documentation:
Return size if the device in bytes.

```pycon
>>> host.block_device("/dev/sda1").size
512110190592
```

methods.name: testinfra.modules.blockdevice.BlockDevice.sector_size
documentation:
Return sector size for the device in bytes.

```pycon
>>> host.block_device("/dev/sda1").sector_size
512
```

methods.name: testinfra.modules.blockdevice.BlockDevice.block_size
documentation:
Return block size for the device in bytes.

```pycon
>>> host.block_device("/dev/sda").block_size
4096
```

methods.name: testinfra.modules.blockdevice.BlockDevice.start_sector
documentation:
Return start sector of the device on the underlying device.

> Usually the value is zero for full devices and is non-zero
> for partitions.

```pycon
>>> host.block_device("/dev/sda1").start_sector
2048
```

```pycon
>>> host.block_device("/dev/md0").start_sector
0
```

methods.name: testinfra.modules.blockdevice.BlockDevice.is_writable
documentation:
Return True if device is writable (have no RO status)

```pycon
>>> host.block_device("/dev/sda").is_writable
True
```

```pycon
>>> host.block_device("/dev/loop1").is_writable
False
```

methods.name: testinfra.modules.blockdevice.BlockDevice.ra
documentation:
Return Read Ahead for the device in 512-bytes sectors.

```pycon
>>> host.block_device("/dev/sda").ra
256
```

Information for block device.

Should be used with sudo or under root.

If device is not a block device, RuntimeError is raised.

Requires root access or sudo

classes.name: testinfra.modules.socket.Socket

methods.name: testinfra.modules.socket.Socket.is_listening
documentation:
Test if socket is listening

```pycon
>>> host.socket("unix:///var/run/docker.sock").is_listening
False
>>> # This HTTP server listen on all ipv4 addresses but not on ipv6
>>> host.socket("tcp://0.0.0.0:80").is_listening
True
>>> host.socket("tcp://:::80").is_listening
False
>>> host.socket("tcp://80").is_listening
False
```

:::{note}
If you don't specify a host for udp and tcp sockets,
then the socket is listening if and only if the
socket listen on **both** all ipv4 and ipv6 addresses
(ie 0.0.0.0 and ::)
:::

methods.name: testinfra.modules.socket.Socket.clients
documentation:
Return a list of clients connected to a listening socket

For tcp and udp sockets a list of pair (address, port) is returned.
For unix sockets a list of None is returned (thus you can make a
len() for counting clients).

```pycon
>>> host.socket("tcp://22").clients
[('2001:db8:0:1', 44298), ('192.168.31.254', 34866)]
>>> host.socket("unix:///var/run/docker.sock")
[None, None, None]
```

methods.name: testinfra.modules.socket.Socket.get_listening_sockets
documentation:
Return a list of all listening sockets

```pycon
>>> host.socket.get_listening_sockets()
['tcp://0.0.0.0:22', 'tcp://:::22', 'unix:///run/systemd/private', ...]
```

Test listening tcp/udp and unix sockets

`socketspec` must be specified as `<protocol>://<host>:<port>`

This module requires the `netstat` command to on the target host.

Example:

> - Unix sockets: `unix:///var/run/docker.sock`
> - All ipv4 and ipv6 tcp sockets on port 22: `tcp://22`
> - All ipv4 sockets on port 22: `tcp://0.0.0.0:22`
> - All ipv6 sockets on port 22: `tcp://:::22`
> - udp socket on 127.0.0.1 port 69: `udp://127.0.0.1:69`

methods.name: testinfra.host.Host.has_command_v
documentation: Return True if `command -v` is available

methods.name: testinfra.host.Host.exists
documentation: Return True if given command exist in $PATH

methods.name: testinfra.host.Host.find_command
documentation:
Return path of given command

raise ValueError if command cannot be found

methods.name: testinfra.host.Host.run
documentation:
Run given command and return rc (exit status), stdout and stderr

```pycon
>>> cmd = host.run("ls -l /etc/passwd")
>>> cmd.rc
0
>>> cmd.stdout
'-rw-r--r-- 1 root root 1790 Feb 11 00:28 /etc/passwd\n'
>>> cmd.stderr
''
>>> cmd.succeeded
True
>>> cmd.failed
False
```

Good practice: always use shell arguments quoting to avoid shell
injection

```pycon
>>> cmd = host.run("ls -l -- %s", "/;echo inject")
CommandResult(
    rc=2, stdout='',
    stderr=(
      'ls: cannot access /;echo inject: No such file or directory\n'),
    command="ls -l '/;echo inject'")
```

methods.name: testinfra.host.Host.run_expect
documentation:
Run command and check it return an expected exit status

```
param expected

A list of expected exit status

raises

AssertionError
```

methods.name: testinfra.host.Host.run_test
documentation:
Run command and check it return an exit status of 0 or 1

:raises: AssertionError

methods.name: testinfra.host.Host.check_output
documentation:
Get stdout of a command which has run successfully

:returns: stdout without trailing newline
:raises: AssertionError

methods.name: testinfra.host.Host.get_host
documentation:
Return a Host instance from `hostspec`

`hostspec` should be like
`<backend_type>://<name>?param1=value1&param2=value2`

Params can also be passed in `**kwargs` (eg. get_host("local://",
sudo=True) is equivalent to get_host("local://?sudo=true"))

Examples:

```
>>> get_host("local://", sudo=True)
>>> get_host("paramiko://user@host", ssh_config="/path/my_ssh_config")
>>> get_host("ansible://all?ansible_inventory=/etc/ansible/inventory")
```

documentation: Run command through ssh command

classes.name: testinfra.backend.ssh.SafeSshBackend
documentation:
Run command using ssh command but try to get a more sane output

When using ssh (or a potentially bugged wrapper) additional output can be
added in stdout/stderr and exit status may not be propagate correctly

To avoid that kind of bugs, we wrap the command to have an output like
this:

TESTINFRA_START;EXIT_STATUS;STDOUT;STDERR;TESTINFRA_END

where STDOUT/STDERR are base64 encoded, then we parse that magic string to
get sanes variables

classes.name: testinfra.backend.base.CommandResult

methods.name: testinfra.backend.base.CommandResult.succeeded
documentation:
Returns whether the command was successful

```pycon
>>> host.run("true").succeeded
True
```

methods.name: testinfra.backend.base.CommandResult.failed
documentation:
Returns whether the command failed

```pycon
>>> host.run("false").failed
True
```

methods.name: testinfra.backend.base.CommandResult.rc
documentation:
Gets the returncode of a command

```pycon
>>> host.run("true").rc
0
```

methods.name: testinfra.backend.base.CommandResult.stdout
documentation:
Gets standard output (stdout) stream of an executed command

```pycon
>>> host.run("mkdir -v new_directory").stdout
mkdir: created directory 'new_directory'
```

methods.name: testinfra.backend.base.CommandResult.stderr
documentation:
Gets standard error (stderr) stream of an executed command

```pycon
>>> host.run("mkdir new_directory").stderr
mkdir: cannot create directory 'new_directory': File exists
```

methods.name: testinfra.backend.base.CommandResult.stdout_bytes
documentation:
Gets standard output (stdout) stream of an executed command as bytes

```pycon
>>> host.run("mkdir -v new_directory").stdout_bytes
b"mkdir: created directory 'new_directory'"
```

methods.name: testinfra.backend.base.CommandResult.stderr_bytes
documentation:
Gets standard error (stderr) stream of an executed command as bytes

```pycon
>>> host.run("mkdir new_directory").stderr_bytes
b"mkdir: cannot create directory 'new_directory': File exists"
```

Object that encapsulates all returned details of the command execution.

Example:

```pycon
>>> cmd = host.run("ls -l /etc/passwd")
>>> cmd.rc
0
>>> cmd.stdout
'-rw-r--r-- 1 root root 1790 Feb 11 00:28 /etc/passwd\n'
>>> cmd.stderr
''
>>> cmd.succeeded
True
>>> cmd.failed
False
```

classes.name: testinfra.backend.base.BaseBackend

methods.name: testinfra.backend.base.BaseBackend.get_connection_type
documentation:
Return the connection backend used as string.

Can be local, paramiko, ssh, docker, salt or ansible

methods.name: testinfra.backend.base.BaseBackend.get_hostname
documentation:
Return the hostname (for testinfra) of the remote or local system

Can be useful for multi-hosts tests:

Example:

```
import requests


def test(TestinfraBackend):
    host = TestinfraBackend.get_hostname()
    response = requests.get("http://" + host)
    assert response.status_code == 200
```

```
$ testinfra --hosts=server1,server2 test.py

test.py::test[paramiko://server1] PASSED
test.py::test[paramiko://server2] PASSED
```
Represent the connection to the remote or local system
